### 3.2.1 Ionic Bonds

We have seen above how atoms can become ions by losing or gaining an electron, which ultimately results in a positively or negatively charged ion, respectively. If you have two atoms that can achieve a stable valence shell by either donating or gaining an electron, the result is a force of attraction which can bond the oppositely charged atoms together via an *ionic bond*. This is most simply illustrated through the bonding of sodium (Na) and chlorine (Cl) atoms to make the compound sodium chloride (NaCl), which is the chemical name for common table salt (see Figure 3.4). It is important to remember that whenever atoms bond via ionic bonds, the net charge of the newly formed compound is always zero.

## Definition

An ionic bond is a force of attraction that bonds oppositely charged ions together, formed when atoms achieve a stable valence shell by either donating or gaining electrons, resulting in positively and negatively charged ions that attract one another.

## Core Principles

- Atoms become ions by losing or gaining electrons, producing positively or negatively charged species respectively.
- When one atom can donate an electron and another can gain an electron to achieve a stable valence shell, the resulting oppositely charged ions are held together by electrostatic attraction.
- The net charge of a compound formed via ionic bonding is always zero, since the positive and negative ionic charges balance out.

## Sub-concepts

- Cation formation: an atom losing one or more electrons to attain a stable valence shell, producing a positively charged ion (e.g., Na → Na⁺).
- Anion formation: an atom gaining one or more electrons to attain a stable valence shell, producing a negatively charged ion (e.g., Cl → Cl⁻).
- Electrostatic attraction between oppositely charged ions as the basis of ionic bonding.

## Practical Applications

- Formation of sodium chloride (NaCl), the chemical name for common table salt, via ionic bonding between sodium (Na) and chlorine (Cl) atoms.
